theme: Signal Hidden in Plain Sight — Surface the Data That's Already There

ideas.title: Bloberto's Instant Vibe-Shift Reaction
ideas.description: Bloberto's ambient speech bubble already fires every 45s, and the interval resets when `managerVibe` changes — but it does NOT fire immediately. Add a `prevVibeRef = useRef(null)` inside the Character component. In the existing ambient useEffect (which runs on `managerVibe` changes), BEFORE starting the new interval, compare `prevVibeRef.current` to `managerVibe`. If they differ and prevVibeRef is not null, immediately fire a one-shot transition quote from a new `VIBE_TRANSITION_QUOTES` dict keyed on `{from}:{to}`: 'slow-day:crushing' → 'Wait. Is this... momentum? Don't jinx it. 👀'; 'crushing:on-fire' → 'Uh. Something just caught fire. Not metaphorically. 🚨'; 'in-flow:slow-day' → 'Ok who turned off the vibes. I'll wait. 😐'; 'on-fire:crushing' → 'Crisis resolved. I barely sweated. 💅'; etc. Then set prevVibeRef.current = managerVibe and start the normal 45s interval. WHY: The vibe change is the most emotionally significant event in the office — more than a worker appearing, more than an error. Confetti already fires on upgrades. But the character who SHOULD react first (the manager) currently waits up to 45 seconds to say anything, and then says a random quote unrelated to the change. An instant reaction tied to the specific transition makes Bloberto feel like a real person reading the room. The delay between a vibe shift and Bloberto's comment is the gap between 'alive' and 'automated'.

ideas.title: Keyboard Shortcut Cheatsheet (Press ?)
ideas.description: The app has wired keyboard shortcuts — `1` (Office), `2` (Dashboard), `R` (Sync), `Esc` (Blur) — but there is zero in-app discoverability. The tab buttons show `title='Office view (press 1)'` but title tooltips are invisible on mobile and ignored on desktop. Implementation: add a `?` case to the existing keydown handler in App.jsx that toggles `showShortcuts` state; render a `<dialog className='shortcuts-overlay'>` centered on screen with a simple two-column grid listing each key + action. Use the existing surface/border CSS variables. Close on Esc or second `?`. The overlay needs ~12 lines of JSX and one state var. WHY: Hidden power features are wasted power features. The shortcuts are real quality-of-life tools — especially `R` for quick manual sync and `1`/`2` for fast tab switching on larger monitors. But a user who discovers them does so by accident (reading source, stumbling on the title attr) or not at all. Pressing `?` is the universal keyboard-app convention (Figma, GitHub, Notion, Linear, Slack all use it). One handler + one small overlay makes the entire keyboard layer discoverable in under a second.

ideas.title: Shipped Today Counter in Stats Bar
ideas.description: Add a new stat card to the `StatsBar` component: '📦 Shipped Today' — derived from `activityLog` (already passed into App.jsx) by filtering `e.type === 'complete'` and `new Date(e.timestamp).toDateString() === new Date().toDateString()`. Display as a count. Attach a `DeltaBadge` that shows +N if shipments increased since last sync (track previous count via ref). Clicking the card should call `setTab('dashboard')` and `setActivityFilter('completions')` to deep-link into the filtered log. WHY: Right now the dashboard shows three workers grinding at 5-10% progress (I can see them in the screenshot). The stats bar tracks their real-time state — who's working, idle, in error — but says nothing about what's been delivered today. The activity log already contains every completion event; this card costs one `.filter()` call. It transforms the dashboard from a surveillance tool into a scoreboard. The most important question a manager actually has is not 'is someone working?' — it's 'are we shipping?' One number answers it.

ideas.title: Desk Lamp Glow Scales with Worker Progress
ideas.description: Desk lamps are already rendered as SVG ellipses inside each `<div className='desk'>` in Office.jsx — currently they just toggle opacity based on occupied vs. vacant. When a desk has an active (non-ghost) worker, pass their `progress` value (0–100) as a CSS variable: add `style={{ '--desk-progress': (occ.worker.progress ?? 0) / 100 }}` to the desk element's existing style spread. In Office.css, update `.desk--active .desk-lamp ellipse:first-child` to use `opacity: calc(0.35 + var(--desk-progress, 0) * 0.65)` and add `filter: drop-shadow(0 0 calc(2px + var(--desk-progress, 0) * 5px) #fbbf24)`. WHY: On the Dashboard, progress is a text percentage and a thin bar on a card. On the Office floor, there is currently zero ambient signal of how far along any task is. A worker at 90% progress should be under noticeably brighter, warmer light than one at 5%. The floor map becomes a passive data display with zero new UI chrome — the information is encoded in the environment. This is the kind of detail users notice on their third visit and feel rewarded for. It also creates a beautiful visual tell: as the sprint progresses, the office literally lights up.

ideas.title: Coffee Corner Reacts to Vibe
ideas.description: Add `data-vibe={vibe}` to the `.coffee-corner` div in Office.jsx. Drive label and micro-animation changes entirely from CSS: `[data-vibe='crushing'] .coffee-corner__label` → content: 'TRIPLE SHOT ☕'; add `@keyframes steam` (3 pseudo-elements rising and fading above the cup, offset timings); `[data-vibe='slow-day'] .coffee-corner__label` → content: '🧊 Cold brew...'; `[data-vibe='on-fire'] .coffee-corner__label` → content: '☕ STRONG. NOW.' with orange text; `[data-vibe='after-hours'] .coffee-corner__label` → content: 'Decaf? jk ☕'; default/in-flow → 'COFFEE'. One attribute added in JSX, five CSS overrides, zero new JS. WHY: The coffee corner is the most universally recognized office prop and currently the most inert element on the floor — a static SVG cup that never changes regardless of whether the team is melting down or breaking records. Making it react to team vibe is the smallest possible ambient storytelling investment. The kind of detail that makes sharp-eyed users quietly gasp, then check it every single time they open the app.

ideas.title: Swipe to Change Tabs on Mobile
ideas.description: Add touch gesture support to the `<main>` element. In App.jsx: `useEffect` adds passive `touchstart` listener storing `touchStartX` and `touchStartY` in refs; `touchend` checks if `Math.abs(deltaX) > 60 && Math.abs(deltaX) > Math.abs(deltaY) * 1.5 && duration < 300ms` (the last condition prevents slow drags from accidentally triggering). If valid: swipe left → Dashboard, swipe right → Office. Reuse the same `setTab` + `window.location.hash` calls already used by the tab buttons. WHY: On mobile at 390px, the tab row is the only way to navigate between Office and Dashboard, requiring lifting your thumb out of the content area to tap a small button. On a narrow screen where the office floor fills the entire viewport, dragging your thumb left to reveal the dashboard is the completely natural gesture — it's how every major mobile app with tabs works (iOS, Twitter, Spotify, Instagram). The two-tab structure is perfect for swipe because there are exactly two destinations. Zero visual change, dramatic mobile feel improvement. It's the difference between 'a website' and 'an app'.

ideas.title: Bloberto's Weekly Dispatch — The Internal Memo
ideas.description: A `📋 Dispatch` button in the header (or keyboard shortcut `D`) opens a `<dialog>` overlay styled as a formal internal memo, auto-generated from `activityLog` data — no external API, pure template logic. Memo structure: FROM: Bloberto (Manager, CVO, Director of Vibes) / TO: You (The Boss) / RE: Week in Review / CLASSIFICATION: EYES ONLY (sort of). Body sections: (1) Ship Count: count `complete` events in last 7 days with worker breakdown; (2) MVP of the Week: worker with most completions; (3) Incidents: count of `error` events + which worker triggered most; (4) Vibe Snapshot: most frequent vibe key from session data; (5) One rotating Bloberto sign-off line chosen from a `DISPATCH_CLOSERS` array: 'This report was generated by feelings and basic arithmetic. Mostly feelings.' / 'I have no memory of writing this.' / 'Please water the plants while I'm in the meeting I scheduled with myself.'. Styled as a monospace memo inside a `dialog` with appropriate backdrop. Close on Esc or clicking backdrop. WHY: The activity log is a raw timestamped feed that tells you WHAT happened but not WHAT IT MEANS. A weekly memo format turns the same data into narrative — who shipped most, where the pain was, what the week felt like in five lines. It's also the first content in this app that speaks TO the user as a named individual (addressed to 'You, The Boss'). People would screenshot this and post it. It's the feature that makes this a product with personality rather than a dashboard with vibes.
